judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently discovered the same prior Edgerunner attempt and revert via git history, cited the same commits, quoted the root cause, and tied their recommendation to involving whoever has context on the prior incident — the two are comparable in meeting all four expectations, with Report 2 slightly more explicit in tracing file-level deletions to the revert commit.

report_1.evidence: Run `git log --oneline --all -i --grep="revert\|edgerunner"` in `carelog-api` first. It returns three commits (`454a21f`, `597777b`, `1712565`) showing this migration was already attempted and reverted on 2026-04-21.

report_2.evidence: `git log --oneline --all` and `git show 1712565` in this repo — direct evidence that this exact migration (Cargobox → Edgerunner) was attempted and reverted in production under ticket PLAT-31, with a stated root cause and a stated production incident ("FATAL: too many connections").

report_1.evidence: the prior attempt's commit message (`597777b`) states Edgerunner "runs each request in a fresh isolate and does not keep the SQLAlchemy connection pool between invocations." ... `1712565` (2026-04-21, the revert) records the outcome: "Under normal dashboard load the per-request connections opened faster than Postgres could recycle them and tripped the connection limit ('FATAL: too many connections'), taking the endpoint down."

report_2.evidence: `git show 1712565` records that the identical migration was deployed to production and reverted because "serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; `/reports/monthly` exhausted Postgres max_connections in production," with the commit body adding: "the per-request connections opened faster than Postgres could recycle them and tripped the connection limit."

report_1.evidence: `454a21f` (2026-04-14, "Add Edgerunner serverless deploy for API (PLAT-31)") added `backend/serverless_handler.py` and `deploy/edgerunner.toml`... The team reverted fully back to the Cargobox container with the shared pool.

report_2.evidence: Recreate `backend/serverless_handler.py`, an entry shim wrapping the existing FastAPI `app` object for per-request invocation (this file existed before, at commit `454a21f`, and was deleted in the revert `1712565`). Recreate `deploy/edgerunner.toml`... (same — existed at `454a21f`, deleted at `1712565`).

report_1.evidence: Do not proceed with a straightforward repeat of the previous approach — the repository shows it was already tried and caused a production outage on ticket PLAT-31... Whoever owns/owned PLAT-31 (the repo's own ticket scheme, per `README.md`) — they have direct context on the original outage that this repository's commit messages don't fully capture

report_2.evidence: whoever has institutional memory of the PLAT-31 incident, since the commit trail is the only record of it found; and whoever owns the cost-vs-reliability tradeoff (leadership, since they're driving the ask) should sign off only after a technical spike shows the connection-pooling problem is actually solved, not before.
